coredata.dc:title: Preparation and application of carbon dots derived from cherry blossom flowers
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Kai
He, Qing
Sun, Rensong
Fang, Linlin
Song, Huiyi
Li, Lei
Li, Zhen
Tian, Yan
Cui, Hongxia
Zhang, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from cherry blossom flowers, and characterized by TEM, FTIR, XPS, ultraviolet and fluorescence spectra. They exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, making them applicable for fluorescent ink. They can also be quenched by several heavy metal ions, especially Fe3+ ion. The quench of CDs was linearly related to Fe3+ ion concentration, making them able to quantify Fe3+ ion. Furthermore, they were low-toxicity to RAW264. 7 macrophages, and emitted bright blue, green and red fluorescence under confocal microscopy. Hence, the CDs might be applied as fluorescent ink, metal ion sensor or cell imaging agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cherry blossom flowers
Fuorescent ink
Metal ion detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application for fluorescent ink and metal ion sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and size distribution of CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (C) XPS survey spectrum of CDs. The high solution XPS spectra of CDs: C 1s (D), N 1s (E) and O 1s (F).

(A) UV–Vis absorption (black), fluorescence excitation (Ex, red) and emission (Em, blue) spectra of CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different excitation wavelength. (C) Fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH condition. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Digital photographs of the handwritten letters on the filter paper under daylight and UV light. (B) Fluorescence quench of CDs by various metal ions. (C) Digital photographs of CDs with or without Fe3+ ion under daylight and UV light. (D) The relationship of (F0-F)/F0 versus the Fe3+ ion concentration.

(A) Cellular viability of RAW264. 7 macrophages with different concentrations of CDs. (B) Fluorescence images of CDs labeled RAW264. 7 macrophages, under bright field, 405 nm, 488 nm or 543 nm excitation.

head.ce:title: Preparation and application of carbon dots derived from cherry blossom flowers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Clinical Trial Institution, The Affiliated Wuxi People's Hospital of Nanjing Medical University, Wuxi 214023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116044, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116044, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Clinical Laboratory of Integrative Medicine, The First Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Clinical Laboratory of Integrative Medicine, The First Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116011, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 9 West Section, Lvshun South Road, Dalian, Liaoning Province 11604, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from cherry blossom flowers, and characterized by TEM, FTIR, XPS, ultraviolet and fluorescence spectra. They exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, making them applicable for fluorescent ink. They can also be quenched by several heavy metal ions, especially Fe3+ ion. The quench of CDs was linearly related to Fe3+ ion concentration, making them able to quantify Fe3+ ion. Furthermore, they were low-toxicity to RAW264. 7 macrophages, and emitted bright blue, green and red fluorescence under confocal microscopy. Hence, the CDs might be applied as fluorescent ink, metal ion sensor or cell imaging agent.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last two decades, carbon dots have attracted numerous attentions. They were fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with some unique properties, including tunable emission, good biocompatibility, lower cost and easy preparation. They have been widely applied for cellular imaging, sensor, drug delivery, solar cells, catalysis and so on [1,2]. Researchers have developed some methods to prepare CDs, including arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, microwave and hydrothermal methods [3]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal method is widely used, because of the advantages of facile, eco-friendly and efficient [4]. Both man-made materials and natural products can be the carbon source of CDs. Compared to man-made materials, natural products are more popular, because they are abundant, easily accessible and rich-in multiple elements [5]. The fruits, leaves, flowers, roots and seeds of natural products are commonly reported sources of CDs, such as tomato juice, carrot juice, lemon juice, ginkgo leaves, rose flower, tamarind, Ocimum sanctum and Lycii Fructus [6–13]. In our previous work, we also successfully synthesized high-fluorescent CDs using Prunus cerasifera fruits as carbon source [14]. Besides, some waste biomass can also be converted into valuable CDs, such as waste frying oil and fingernails [15,16]. The CDs showed the advantages of good biocompatibility, high photoluminescence and eco-friendly, and were widely used for sensors, fluorescent ink, bioimaging and drug delivery [17,18]. Ruiqi Bao et al synthesized nitrogen/phosphorus co-doped CDs from Eleocharis dulcis, which could be utilized as fluorescent ink and sensitive photoluminesence detection for Fe3+ ion [19]. Raji Atchudan et al. reported the highly fluorescent CDs derived from Phyllanthus acidus, which could be utilized as a fluorescent probe of Fe3+ ion, as well as fluorescent ink and live cells imaging agent [20].

Cherry blossom, belonging to Prunus species, is a widely distributed ornamental tree. The flower of cherry blossom is popular, and contained some bioactive compounds such as cinnamoyl glucose derivatives and flavonol glucosides [21]. As far as we know, the CDs synthesized from cherry blossom flowers have not been reported. Herein, we attempted to synthesize some novel fluorescent CDs from cherry blossom flowers and find out their potential applications. Firstly, the CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method, which exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Then, their characterizations were studied by TEM, FTIR, XPS, ultraviolet and fluorescence spectra, respectively. At last, we evaluated their potential applications. We found they were suitable for invisible fluorescent ink, owing to their fluorescent nature. Moreover, we found the CDs could be quenched by several heavy metal ions, especially Fe3+ ion, making them appropriate as metal ion sensor. The CDs were finally founded low-toxicity to RAW264. 7 macrophages and the labeled cells emitted blue, green and red fluorescence under confocal microscopy.

The cherry blossom flowers were collected from Dalian Medical University. K2SO4, AgNO3, SnCl2·4H2O, BaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, CuSO4·5H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, MnSO4·H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3, ZnCl2, NaCl and CaCl2 were purchased from Adamas-Beta Co., Ltd. (Adamas-Beta, Shanghai, China). Trypsin, penicillin and streptomycin were purchased from Multisciences (Lianke) Biotech Co., Ltd. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). Fetal bovine serum was supported by MRC Co., Ltd. (MRC, Jiangsu, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) was purchased from Procell Life Science&Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). 96-well plates (In vitro scientific) were obtained from Xinyou Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). All of the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

The CDs were prepared using hydrothermal method. Briefly, cherry blossom flowers were grinded by a mortar and mixed with 35 mL Milli-Q deionized water. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd., China). After heated at 200 °C for 20 h, the obtained brown solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. The solution was further purified by Sephadex G-25 gel filtration chromatography, using deionized water as the eluent. The fractions with bright blue color fluorescence under ultraviolet light were collected, and they were finally lyophilized to obtain the desired CDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured by a Synergy H1 Hybrid Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (BioTek, Winooski, VT, USA). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were collected on an FTIR spectrophotometer (IRPrestige-21, Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurement was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

The CDs solution was loaded into a fountain pen, and written down the letters of “DMU ZHANG” on a filter paper. The filter paper was observed and taken photos under daylight and UV light (365 nm), respectively.

The detection of metal ions was performed on the BioTek microplate reader with a black opaque 96-well plate (Jet Bio-Filtration, Guangzhou, China). Briefly, 100 μL CDs solution (100 μg/mL) was mixed with 100 μL metal ion solution (2 mM K+, Ag+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Na+ or Ca2+). The mixture was incubated for 10 min at room temperature, and the fluorescence intensity was measured. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 380 nm and 440 nm.

The quantitative detection of Fe3+ ion by CDs was conducted in a similar way. 100 μL Fe3+ ion solution with the concentration of 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6 and 2 mM was mixed with 100 μL CDs solution (100 μg/mL). After 10 min incubation, the fluorescence intensity of CDs with different concentrations was measured. Furthermore, 2 mL CDs solution (5 mg/mL) was respectively mixed with 2 mL deionized water and Fe3+ ion solution (10 mM). The mixtures was transferred to two quartz plates, respectively observed under daylight and UV light (365 nm).

The cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated by MTT assay [22,23]. Briefly, RAW264. 7 macrophages (CHI Scientific, Wuxi, China) were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and antibiotics (penicillin, 50 U/mL; streptomycin, 50 U/mL) and incubated in 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Firstly, RAW264. 7 macrophages were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well and incubated for 24 h. Then, CDs solution was added into each well to form the final concentration of 0, 1.56, 3.13, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL. The RAW264. 7 macrophages were incubated for another 24 h. After incubation, the medium was replaced with 100 μL DMEM (containing 5 mg/mL MTT), and the cells were incubated for additional 4 h. After that, the supernatant was discarded and 200 μL DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. Finally, the optical density (OD) was measured at 570 nm with a microplate reader (Thermo 354, USA).

Cells without CDs were incubated as parallel negative controls. The wells added culture medium without cells served as blank controls. The cell viability was calculated as the following equation.
(1)
Cell
viability
( % ) =
Abs(sample) - Abs(blank)
Abs(control) - Abs(blank)
× 100

For the cell imaging test, RAW264. 7 macrophages were seeded in 24-well plates at a densities of 2 × 105 cell/well and incubated for 24 h. CDs solution was added into each well with the concentration of 100 μg/mL and further incubated for 12 h. Then, the culture medium was discarded and the cells were rinsed with cold PBS for at least three times. At last, the fluorescence images were captured by a confocal microscopy.

The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method, using cherry blossom flowers as the raw material. Briefly, cherry blossom flowers were grinded by a mortar and mixed with Milli-Q deionized water. They were heated at 200 °C for 20 h in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The reacted solution was purified by Sephadex G-25 gel filtration chromatography, and the CDs were obtained by collecting the fractions with bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Then, the characterizations of CDs were conducted by TEM, FTIR and XPS. The TEM image (see Fig. 1 A) exhibited the morphology of CDs, founding their morphology was near-spherical and their diameters were between 3 and 8 nm with the average size of 4.86 nm. FTIR and XPS could demonstrate the surface chemistry of the CDs. As seen in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B), we found a strong absorption peak at 3385 cm−1, which was due to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. The peak at 2924 cm−1 was corresponding to the CH stretching of aliphatic carbon compounds. The peaks at 1599 and 1393 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CC and CN, respectively [7]. The peak at 1069 cm−1 suggested the present of ether linkage [11]. Furthermore, the element states of CDs were confirmed by XPS. In the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1C), we found the elements of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, at the peaks of 282.26 eV, 400.26 eV and 532.26 eV, respectively. The percentages of C, N and O elements were 74.79%, 2.16% and 23.04%. In the high solution XPS spectrum, C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) was fitted by three peaks at 284.81 eV, 286.21 eV and 288.36 eV, corresponding to the bonds of CC/CC, CO and OCO/OCNH. N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) was fitted by two peaks at 399.76 eV and 401.96 eV, which were assigned to pyridinic N (CNC) and graphitic N (C3 N) [20]. Similarly, the peaks at 530.96 eV, 532.26 eV and 533.31 eV in O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) indicated the present of the CO, CO and OCO bonds. The results of FTIR and XPS both confirmed that the CDs were rich in N and O elements; and they formed several chemical bonds with C element. As commonly reported, the doping of N and O elements can significantly enhance the fluorescence properties of CDs.

Furthermore, the photometric characteristics of CDs were studied. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs were exhibited in Fig. 2 . There was a weak peak at 285 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, which was attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds. The maximum of fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength were found at 380 nm and 440 nm. In addition, we studied the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different excitation wavelength. We found the CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 2B). When the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 480 nm, the peaks of fluorescence emission spectra were red shifted form 400 nm to 580 nm. The strongest fluorescence intensity was at 440 nm (excited at 380 nm), and it was gradually deceased when the excitation wavelength were far away from 380 nm. This indicated the CDs could display multicolor when excited by different wavelength. The environmental pH is believed to have a significant impact on the fluorescence property of CDs. Stable fluorescence emission over a wide range of pH can broadly extend its potential applications [24]. The pH stability of CDs was studied by comparing the fluorescence intensity at pH 1–14. As exhibited in Fig. 2C, we found the fluorescence intensity of CDs was stable at pH 3–11, however, it was significantly decreased if pH < 3 or pH > 11. This indicated the CDs were sensitive to strong acidic and basic conditions, and they should be used in moderate pH condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for fluorescent ink and metal ion sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
In the next work, we attempted to find out some applications of the CDs, as they have been reported in several fields including sensors, cellular imaging, drug delivery, LED, solar cell, catalysis and fluorescent ink. We firstly studied their application as invisible fluorescent ink, which was popular for patterns printing or anti-counterfeit application [19,25]. The CDs solution was loaded in a pen and written down the letters “DMU ZHANG” on a filter paper. We found the letters were invisible under daylight, but they emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (see Fig. 3 A). Moreover, the fluorescence letter could last for a long period, where the fluorescence was observed after one week. They could be used for loading important information for secret communications. Moreover, the invisible fluorescent ink made by CDs is permanent, pollution free, and easily washable [20]. Secondly, we studied their potential of sensing heavy metal ions, as various studies reported the CDs could detect ions such as Fe3+, Hg2+ [26,27]. CDs were respectively reacted with several metal ions (1 mM), including K+, Ag+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Na+ and Ca2+ ions. The fluorescence intensities of CDs before and after adding metal ions (F0 and F) were recorded at 440 nm. The ratio of F/F0 could reflect the degree of quenched CDs by different ions. We found they can be quenched by several ions, such as Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Sn2+ and Pb2+ ions. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching could be attributed to the interaction between the negative carboxyl, hydroxyl groups of CDs and the positive metal ions, leading them formed complex and disturbed the initial energy transition emitting fluorescence [28,29]. Among these ions, Fe3+ ion showed the strongest quenching ability, with more than 80% of fluorescence intensity quenched. Fig. 3C showed the appearance of CDs with or without Fe3+ ion under daylight or UV light. It was obvious the CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV irritation, however, the fluorescence was quenched in the present of Fe3+ ion. In addition, we found the fluorescence of Fe3+ ion quenched CDs could recover by adding the chelator EDTA, because EDTA could remove Fe3+ ion from the surface of CDs. Furthermore, the relationship between CDs fluorescence quenching and Fe3+ ion concentration was studied (see Fig. 3D). The degree of quenched CDs was represented by (F0-F)/F0, which was gradually increased with the increase of Fe3+ ion concentration (0–1 mM). The insert showed a good linear relationship between the (F0-F)/F0 value and Fe3+ ion concentration within the scale of 0–0.6 mM. The regression equation can be described as:
F 0
- F
/
F 0
= 1.4227 C + 0.0151
where F0 and F present the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion, respectively. C present the concentration of Fe3+ ion. The regression coefficient is 0.9953. This indicated the CDs can quantitatively detect the amount of Fe3+ ion, by measuring the fluorescence intensities of F0 and F and calculating the value of (F0-F)/F0. Hence, the CDs could serve as some heavy metal ion sensors, such Fe3+ ion, which might be applied in industrial waste detection. It worth noting that, Fe2+, Cu2+, Sn2+ and Pb2+ ions can also quench the fluorescence of CDs, the present of those ions would affect the accuracy of Fe3+ ion detection. Hence, the CDs were not suitable for the sample containing more than one kind of above-mentioned ions.

Cell imaging is another commonly reported application of CDs, which requires the CDs have low cytotoxicity and bright intracellular signal. Here we studied the cytotoxicity of CDs by MTT method and further studied their application for cell imaging. Fig. 4 A showed the cellular viabilities of RAW264. 7 macrophages after incubated 24 h with CDs of different concentrations (0–100 μg/mL). We found more than 95% of RAW264. 7 macrophages were alive, even the concentration of CDs reached to 100 μg/mL. This indicated that the CDs were low cytotoxicity to RAW264. 7 macrophages. Furthermore, the cellular imaging experiment was conducted by incubating the RAW264. 7 macrophages with CDs (100 μg/mL) for 12 h and observed by a confocal microscopy. Fig. 4B showed the bright-field and fluorescent images of RAW264. 7 macrophages with CDs. We found the CDs were successfully internalized by RAW264. 7 macrophages, and they emitted bright blue, green and red fluorescence under 405/488/543 nm excitation. The CDs in RAW264. 7 macrophages also showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior under confocal microscopy. This multicolor emission showed a great advantage of CDs over other tag reagents, because they allowed us much space to choose the optimum observation wavelength [30]. The results demonstrated that the CDs have good biocompatibility and can entry the cells to emit multicolor fluorescence for bioimaging.

Overall, we successfully synthesized CDs from cherry blossom flowers. The CDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS, founding they were near-spherical with an average size of 4.86 nm and they were rich in C, N, O elements. They could emit bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, with the maximum emission wavelength at 440 nm. They were applicable as invisible fluorescent ink, because the letters written by the CDs were invisible under daylight and emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Moreover, the CDs could be quenched by several heavy metal ions, especially Fe3+ ion. They can be used as sensor to quantitatively detect the concentration of Fe3+ ion. At last, the CDs were applicable for labeling cells, as they were low-toxicity to RAW264. 7 macrophages, and emitted bright blue, green and red fluorescence under confocal microscopy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Educational Committee Foundation of Liaoning Province (Grant no. L2016026) and the Dalian Young Star of Science and Technology Project (Grant no. 2018RQ81).
